revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白山信仰]][[zh:白山信仰]]
[[File:Hakusan Shrine in Murodo 2008-7-1.jpg|300px|thumbnail|View of {{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社#奥宮|Shirayama Hime Shrine=Shirayama Hime Shrine|lt=Hakusan Okumiya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirayama Hime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Gozengamine|ja|御前峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozengamine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from {{ill|Murodō|en|Murodō|qid=Q11452989|lt=Murodo (Tateyama)|1=Murodō}}]] '''Hakusan Faith''' (白山信仰, Hakusan Shinkō) is a form of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} centered around {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|qid=Q2639098|lt=Mount Hakusan|1=Mount Haku}}, which spans the former provinces of {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|qid=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}}, {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|qid=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province|1=Echizen Province}}, and {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|qid=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}).
== Formation == Hakusan, known as "越のしらやま" (Koshi no Shirayama) in ancient poetry, is considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Holy Mountains|ja|日本三名山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Holy Mountains|1=Three Famous Mountains of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11507103}}, alongside {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} and {{ill|Mount Tate|en|Mount Tate|qid=Q2977103|lt=Tateyama|1=Mount Tate}}. The abundant waters flowing from Hakusan nourished the surrounding rivers, sustaining agriculture and human life. This led to its veneration as a life-giving deity, worshipped as a {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|qid=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}} and agricultural deity. The mountain itself was revered as a sacred body (神体), and its worship spread among the communities along the {{ill|Kuzuryū River|en|Kuzuryū River|qid=Q6449325|lt=Kuzuryū River|1=Kuzuryū River}}, {{ill|Tedori River|en|Tedori River|qid=Q11496932|lt=Tedori River|1=Tedori River}}, and {{ill|Nagara River|en|Nagara River|qid=Q1194094|lt=Nagara River|1=Nagara River}}.
During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, ascetic practitioners (修験者) began establishing sacred sites for {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} across Japan. {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|qid=Q2388439|lt=Taichō|1=Taichō}}, a Buddhist monk, climbed Hakusan and formally established its religious practices, transforming the primal Hakusan faith into a structured Shugendō tradition.
== Primary Regions == Research on the historical transmission of Hakusan Faith is extensive, but geographical studies on its expansion remain relatively scarce.<ref name="kaihou">[http://www.osaka-kyoiku.ac.jp/~shakai/chiri/kaihou/nishide.pdf "Hakusan Faith in Southern Kaga Region"] by Kanako Nishide (Osaka University of Education, Geography Bulletin, Vol. 55, 2008)</ref>
However, the distribution of {{ill|Hakusan Shrines|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrines|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11579587}} serves as an essential indicator of its spread. Studies confirm that the southern Kaga region ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en|Kaga, Ishikawa|qid=Q120103|lt=Kaga|1=Kaga, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Komatsu, Ishikawa|en|Komatsu, Ishikawa|qid=Q646898|lt=Komatsu|1=Komatsu, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Nomi, Ishikawa|en|Nomi, Ishikawa|qid=Q658912|lt=Nomi|1=Nomi, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Hakusan, Ishikawa|en|Hakusan, Ishikawa|qid=Q696407|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Kawakita, Ishikawa|en|Kawakita, Ishikawa|qid=Q1347231|lt=Kawakita|1=Kawakita, Ishikawa}}, and {{ill|Nonoichi, Ishikawa|en|Nonoichi, Ishikawa|qid=Q1354896|lt=Nonoichi|1=Nonoichi, Ishikawa}}) played a central role in Hakusan worship.<ref name="kaihou" /> This area contains Hakusan itself, major tributaries of the Tedori River, {{ill|Kakehashi River|en|Kakehashi River|qid=Q11538771|lt=Katsura River|1=Kakehashi River}}, {{ill|Dōbachi River|ja|動橋川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōbachi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Daishōji River|ja|大聖寺川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishōji River|1=Daishōji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11438532}}, as well as numerous Hakusan-related temples, including the {{ill|Chūgū Hachi-in|ja|中宮八院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūgū Hachi-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504600|1=Chūgū Hachi-in Temple}}, {{ill|Hakusan Goin|ja|白山五院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Goin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504604|1=Hakusan Go-in Temple}}, and {{ill|Sanga-dera|ja|三箇寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanga-dera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="kaihou" />
== History ==
=== Ancient Period === According to legend, in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}'s reign (91 BCE), a distant {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} (遥拝所) was established on {{ill|Funaokayama|ja|舟岡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Funaokayama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to venerate Hakusan. The enshrined deities included {{ill|Kikurihime|en|Kikurihime|qid=Q11619891|lt=Kikurihime no Kami|1=Kikurihime}}, {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}}, and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}.
In the 12th year of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|qid=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}'s reign (82 CE), the {{ill|Hakusan Nakai Shrine|ja|白山中居神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Nakai Shrine|qq=}} was founded in {{ill|Itoshiro|ja|石徹白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itoshiro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and in the 9th year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}'s reign (465 CE), imperial envoys conducted official rites there.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hakusan Nakai Shrine History |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/911332/1/8 |website=NDL Digital Collections |publisher=National Diet Library |editor=Uesugi Tomimaro |year=1912}}</ref>
=== Development of Hakusan Worship === In 717 CE ({{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|qid=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō era|1=Yōrō}} 1), Taichō, while meditating on Hakusan’s main peak, {{ill|Gozengamine|ja|御前峰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozengamine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, witnessed the appearance of the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} in {{ill|Midorigaike|ja|翠ヶ池|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midorigaike|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This deity identified itself as {{ill|Kuzuryū|ja|九頭龍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuzuryū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九頭竜伝承|1=Kuzuryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13577962}}, an incarnation of Izanami, and revealed itself as Hakusan Myōri Daibosatsu (白山妙理大菩薩).<ref name="denki">『加賀白山伝記之事』</ref><ref name="oshou">『泰澄和尚伝記』</ref>
In 718 CE (Yōrō 2), Taichō built a shrine on Gozengamine, enshrining Hakusan Myōri Daigongen.
=== Medieval Period === During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, three pilgrimage routes (禅定道) were established from Kaga, Echizen, and Mino provinces. These routes led to three primary Hakusan worship centers (三馬場): - {{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|en|Shirayama Hime Shrine|qid=Q704702|lt=Hakusan Hime Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} (Kaga) - {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|en|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|qid=Q11483206|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|1=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine}} (Echizen) - {{ill|Nagataki Hakusan Shrine|ja|長滝白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagataki Hakusan Shrine|1=Nagataki Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11653351}} (Mino)
These were later associated with temple complexes: Hakusan-dera, Heisen-ji, and {{ill|Nagataki-dera|ja|長瀧寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagataki-dera|1=Chōryū-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11653375}}. By 820 CE ({{ill|Tenchō|en|Tenchō|qid=Q1373138|lt=Tenchō era|1=Tenchō}} 9), these became subordinate to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}.
Hakusan faith spread widely, especially during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, when Hakusan pilgrimage became an alternative to the interrupted routes of {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|qid=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.<ref>Gorai Shigeru, "History of Japanese Buddhism", Kadokawa, 1989, ISBN 978-4047031890.</ref>
=== Edo and Meiji Periods === During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Hakusan worship flourished under {{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 前田氏}} patronage. However, the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}} saw the abolition of {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}, leading to the forced separation of shrines and temples ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}). Hakusan shrines were restructured, and many temples were abolished.
== Main Hakusan Shrines == Hakusan shrines exist nationwide, with over 2,700 recorded. The most notable are: - {{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|en|Shirayama Hime Shrine|qid=Q704702|lt=Hakusan Hime Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} (Ishikawa) - Head shrine - {{ill|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|en|Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|qid=Q11483206|lt=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine|1=Heisenji Hakusan Shrine}} (Fukui) - {{ill|Nagataki Hakusan Shrine|ja|長滝白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagataki Hakusan Shrine|1=Nagataki Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11653351}} (Gifu)
== References == {{reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == - {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}} - {{ill|Eta-dera|ja|穢多寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eta-dera|1=etaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11597185}}

[[zh:%E7%99%BD%E5%B1%B1%E4%BF%A1%E4%BB%B0]] [[ja:白山信仰]]
